main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Student-Teacher (ST) method for UMA","Contrastive Alignment (CA) method for UMA","Cross-modal Transfer Through Time (C3T) method for UMA"],"problems":["Unsupervised Modality Adaptation (UMA) for Human Action Recognition, where the test modality is not labeled during training","Transferring knowledge from a well-documented modality (e.g., RGB video) to a new, unlabeled modality (e.g., IMU)","Improving robustness and generalization of human action recognition models to new sensor modalities with no labeled data"],"datasets":["Four diverse human action recognition datasets with RGB and IMU modalities (exact names not specified in the provided content)"],"metrics":["Top-1 Accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Top-1 Accuracy (C3T vs. other UMA methods)","value":"C3T outperforms other UMA methods by at least 8% margin on all datasets"},{"metric":"Top-1 Accuracy (C3T UMA vs. RGB supervised)","value":"C3T in UMA setting outperforms RGB unimodal supervised setting"}],"novelty_claims":["Formalization and exploration of the Unsupervised Modality Adaptation (UMA) setting, where the test modality is completely unlabeled during training","Development of the C3T method, which introduces a novel mechanism for aligning signals across time-varying latent vectors using temporal convolutions and a shared self-attention head","Demonstration that C3T achieves superior robustness to temporal noise (time-shift, misalignment, time-dilation) compared to other methods","Empirical finding that C3T can outperform even supervised unimodal models in the UMA setting, highlighting its effectiveness for cross-modal transfer","Provision of insights into latent space alignment for effective cross-modal knowledge transfer, potentially generalizable to other multi-modal learning tasks"]}

methods: Student-Teacher (ST) method for UMA
Contrastive Alignment (CA) method for UMA
Cross-modal Transfer Through Time (C3T) method for UMA
problems: Unsupervised Modality Adaptation (UMA) for Human Action Recognition, where the test modality is not labeled during training
Transferring knowledge from a well-documented modality (e.g., RGB video) to a new, unlabeled modality (e.g., IMU)
Improving robustness and generalization of human action recognition models to new sensor modalities with no labeled data
datasets: Four diverse human action recognition datasets with RGB and IMU modalities (exact names not specified in the provided content)

results.metric: Top-1 Accuracy (C3T vs. other UMA methods)
results.value: C3T outperforms other UMA methods by at least 8% margin on all datasets

results.metric: Top-1 Accuracy (C3T UMA vs. RGB supervised)
results.value: C3T in UMA setting outperforms RGB unimodal supervised setting

novelty_claims: Formalization and exploration of the Unsupervised Modality Adaptation (UMA) setting, where the test modality is completely unlabeled during training
Development of the C3T method, which introduces a novel mechanism for aligning signals across time-varying latent vectors using temporal convolutions and a shared self-attention head
Demonstration that C3T achieves superior robustness to temporal noise (time-shift, misalignment, time-dilation) compared to other methods
Empirical finding that C3T can outperform even supervised unimodal models in the UMA setting, highlighting its effectiveness for cross-modal transfer
Provision of insights into latent space alignment for effective cross-modal knowledge transfer, potentially generalizable to other multi-modal learning tasks

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Instance-based transfer learning (mapping instances of one modality to another)","Feature-based transfer learning (relating modalities in an intermediate latent space)","Systematic literature review of IMU-based cross-modal transfer for HAR"],"problems":["Limited use of IMU data in human activity recognition (HAR)","Lack of cross-modal transfer learning approaches in HAR","Difficulty in utilizing knowledge from multiple sensor modalities for HAR","Inconsistent terminology in the literature regarding transfer learning, domain adaptation, representation learning, sensor fusion, and multimodal learning"],"datasets":[],"metrics":[],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["This is the first survey to systematically review IMU-based cross-modal transfer learning in human activity recognition (HAR).","The paper categorizes HAR tasks by time and abstractness and compares various types of multimodal HAR datasets.","The paper distinguishes and clarifies inconsistently used terms in the literature, such as transfer learning, domain adaptation, representation learning, sensor fusion, and multimodal learning, and describes how cross-modal learning fits with these concepts.","The survey provides a structured categorization of cross-modal transfer approaches into instance-based and feature-based transfer."]}

methods: Instance-based transfer learning (mapping instances of one modality to another)
Feature-based transfer learning (relating modalities in an intermediate latent space)
Systematic literature review of IMU-based cross-modal transfer for HAR
problems: Limited use of IMU data in human activity recognition (HAR)
Lack of cross-modal transfer learning approaches in HAR
Difficulty in utilizing knowledge from multiple sensor modalities for HAR
Inconsistent terminology in the literature regarding transfer learning, domain adaptation, representation learning, sensor fusion, and multimodal learning
novelty_claims: This is the first survey to systematically review IMU-based cross-modal transfer learning in human activity recognition (HAR).
The paper categorizes HAR tasks by time and abstractness and compares various types of multimodal HAR datasets.

The paper distinguishes and clarifies inconsistently used terms in the literature, such as transfer learning, domain adaptation, representation learning, sensor fusion, and multimodal learning, and describes how cross-modal learning fits with these concepts.
The survey provides a structured categorization of cross-modal transfer approaches into instance-based and feature-based transfer.

methods: Video Masked Autoencoder (VideoMAE)
Customized tube masking with extremely high masking ratio (90%-95%)
Asymmetric encoder-decoder architecture for video reconstruction
problems: Data inefficiency in self-supervised video pre-training (SSVP)
Difficulty of training vanilla vision transformers (ViT) on small-scale video datasets without extra data or pre-trained models
Information leakage in masked video modeling due to temporal redundancy and correlation
Domain shift between pre-training and target datasets in SSVP
novelty_claims: First masked video pre-training framework using plain ViT backbones without extra data or pre-trained models.
Customized tube masking with extremely high masking ratio (90%-95%) to address temporal redundancy and information leakage in videos.
Demonstrates that VideoMAE is a data-efficient learner, achieving strong performance with as few as 3.5k videos.
Finds that data quality is more important than data quantity for SSVP, especially under domain shift.
Significantly outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods trained from scratch or with contrastive learning under the same data constraints.

methods: Two-Stream Inflated 3D ConvNet (I3D)
Pre-training on Kinetics dataset followed by fine-tuning on smaller datasets
Re-implementation and evaluation of representative neural network architectures for action recognition
problems: Limited size of existing action recognition datasets (UCF-101, HMDB-51) hindering progress in video architecture development

novelty_claims: Introduction of the Kinetics Human Action Video Dataset, which is two orders of magnitude larger than previous datasets and contains 400 action classes with over 400 clips per class.
Proposal of the Two-Stream Inflated 3D ConvNet (I3D), which inflates 2D ConvNet filters and pooling kernels into 3D to learn spatio-temporal features from video.
Demonstration that pre-training on Kinetics significantly boosts performance on smaller action recognition benchmarks (UCF-101, HMDB-51), especially with the I3D model.
Analysis of transfer learning behavior for action recognition architectures pre-trained on large-scale video data.

methods: View adaptive recurrent neural network (RNN) with LSTM architecture
Self-regulated view adaptation scheme integrated into end-to-end LSTM network
problems: Skeleton-based human action recognition
Large view variations in captured human actions
View invariance for skeleton-based action recognition
novelty_claims: Proposes a novel view adaptation scheme that automatically regulates observation viewpoints during action recognition from skeleton data.
Introduces a view adaptive RNN with LSTM architecture that enables end-to-end learning of optimal viewpoints for action recognition.
The view adaptation is learned by the network itself, rather than relying on human-defined pre-processing or criteria.
The approach maintains action continuity and transforms skeletons to more consistent viewpoints, improving recognition performance.

methods: 3D Convolutional Neural Networks (3D CNNs) within the residual learning framework (3D ResNets)
